title: Algorithms & Patterns
description: The core algorithmic patterns that appear in ~80% of FAANG coding interviews. Master these skeletons and you can adapt them to novel problems on the fly.

sections.title: Binary Search — Left & Right Bound Variants
sections.description: Binary search is O(log N) but has many variants. FAANG interviews frequently ask for leftmost/rightmost occurrence, not just existence. Master all three variants.
sections.codeBlock:
# Variant 1: Exact match — returns index or -1
def binary_search(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2  # avoids integer overflow (good habit)
        if arr[mid] == target: return mid
        elif arr[mid] < target: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid - 1
    return -1

# Variant 2: Left bound — first index where arr[i] >= target
def left_bound(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr)
    while lo < hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if arr[mid] < target: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid
    return lo  # lo == hi; arr[lo] is first element >= target

# Variant 3: Right bound — last index where arr[i] <= target
def right_bound(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr)
    while lo < hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if arr[mid] <= target: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid
    return lo - 1  # last position <= target

# Python stdlib: bisect_left = left_bound, bisect_right = right_bound
from bisect import bisect_left, bisect_right
count = bisect_right(arr, target) - bisect_left(arr, target)  # frequency

sections.title: Two-Pointer & Sliding Window
sections.description: Two-pointer reduces O(N²) brute force to O(N). Sliding window is a specialized two-pointer for subarrays/substrings. Know which to use: two-pointer for sorted arrays or finding pairs; sliding window for subarray constraints.
sections.codeBlock:
# --- Two-Pointer: Two Sum in sorted array ---
def two_sum_sorted(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while lo < hi:
        s = arr[lo] + arr[hi]
        if s == target: return (lo, hi)
        elif s < target: lo += 1
        else: hi -= 1
    return None

# --- Sliding Window: Longest substring without repeating chars ---
def length_of_longest_substring(s: str) -> int:
    seen = {}      # char -> last index
    left = best = 0
    for right, ch in enumerate(s):
        if ch in seen and seen[ch] >= left:
            left = seen[ch] + 1   # shrink window past last occurrence
        seen[ch] = right
        best = max(best, right - left + 1)
    return best

# --- Sliding Window: Minimum window substring (hard) ---
from collections import Counter
def min_window(s: str, t: str) -> str:
    need, have = Counter(t), Counter()
    formed, required = 0, len(need)
    lo, best = 0, (float('inf'), 0, 0)
    for hi, ch in enumerate(s):
        have[ch] += 1
        if ch in need and have[ch] == need[ch]: formed += 1
        while formed == required:
            if hi - lo + 1 < best[0]: best = (hi - lo + 1, lo, hi)
            have[s[lo]] -= 1
            if s[lo] in need and have[s[lo]] < need[s[lo]]: formed -= 1
            lo += 1
    return s[best[1]:best[2]+1] if best[0] != float('inf') else ''

sections.title: Dynamic Programming — Memoization vs Tabulation
sections.description:
Memoization (top-down): Recursive + cache. Cleaner code, only computes needed subproblems. Risk: recursion depth limit, function call overhead.
Tabulation (bottom-up): Iterative, fills table in order. No recursion overhead, easier space optimization. Preferred in production/interviews for large inputs.

sections.title: Backtracking — Permutations, Subsets, N-Queens
sections.description: Backtracking = DFS + undo. The skeleton is always: choose → explore → unchoose. Key optimization: prune early to cut branches. Time complexity is usually O(N!) or O(2^N) — mention this upfront.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Permutations — O(N * N!) ===
def permutations(nums):
    result, path = [], []
    used = [False] * len(nums)
    def backtrack():
        if len(path) == len(nums):
            result.append(path[:]); return
        for i, num in enumerate(nums):
            if used[i]: continue
            used[i] = True; path.append(num)   # choose
            backtrack()                          # explore
            path.pop(); used[i] = False          # unchoose
    backtrack()
    return result

# === Subsets (Power Set) — O(N * 2^N) ===
def subsets(nums):
    result, path = [[]], []
    def backtrack(start):
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            path.append(nums[i])
            result.append(path[:])
            backtrack(i + 1)        # elements after i only (avoid duplicates)
            path.pop()
    backtrack(0)
    return result

# === N-Queens — O(N!) ===
def solve_n_queens(n):
    result, queens = [], set()
    cols, diag1, diag2 = set(), set(), set()
    def backtrack(row):
        if row == n:
            result.append(list(queens)); return
        for col in range(n):
            if col in cols or (row-col) in diag1 or (row+col) in diag2:
                continue  # prune: under attack
            queens.add((row, col)); cols.add(col)
            diag1.add(row-col); diag2.add(row+col)
            backtrack(row + 1)
            queens.discard((row, col)); cols.discard(col)
            diag1.discard(row-col); diag2.discard(row+col)
    backtrack(0)
    return result

sections.title: BFS & DFS — Graph/Tree Traversal Templates
sections.description: BFS uses a queue → shortest path in unweighted graphs. DFS uses a stack (or recursion) → connected components, cycle detection, topological sort. Memorize both templates cold.

sections.title: Greedy Algorithms — When & Why
sections.description: Greedy works when a locally optimal choice leads to a globally optimal solution. Prove correctness via exchange argument: assume an optimal solution differs — show swapping to greedy's choice doesn't worsen it. Common greedy problems: interval scheduling, Dijkstra, Huffman coding, jump game.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Interval Scheduling (Activity Selection) ===
# Greedy: always pick the activity that ends earliest
def max_non_overlapping(intervals):
    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])  # sort by end time
    count, end = 0, float('-inf')
    for start, finish in intervals:
        if start >= end:   # no overlap
            count += 1
            end = finish
    return count

# === Jump Game II — minimum jumps to reach end ===
def jump(nums):
    jumps = current_end = farthest = 0
    for i in range(len(nums) - 1):
        farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i])
        if i == current_end:    # must use a jump here
            jumps += 1
            current_end = farthest
    return jumps

# === Merge Intervals ===
def merge_intervals(intervals):
    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
    merged = [intervals[0]]
    for start, end in intervals[1:]:
        if start <= merged[-1][1]:  # overlap
            merged[-1][1] = max(merged[-1][1], end)
        else:
            merged.append([start, end])
    return merged

details:
Pattern Recognition Cheat Sheet: Array + sorted → binary search or two-pointer. Substring/subarray + constraint → sliding window. Tree/graph traversal → BFS (shortest path) or DFS (connected components, paths). Optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems → DP. Locally optimal choice → greedy (verify with exchange argument).

Binary Search on Answer: Many optimization problems (minimize max, maximize min) can be solved by binary searching on the answer value and checking feasibility. E.g., 'Koko Eating Bananas', 'Capacity to Ship Packages', 'Split Array Largest Sum'.

Monotonic Stack insight: Whenever you see 'next greater element', 'previous smaller element', or 'largest rectangle in histogram', think monotonic stack — maintains elements in sorted order by popping as you go. O(N) overall since each element is pushed and popped at most once.
